beats.text: Before two variables can be related, each one must be described along two axes: where it sits and how spread out it is.
The mean is the balance point and the median is the middle value once sorted; they agree on symmetric data and diverge on skewed data.
Variance is the average squared distance from the mean, with the divisor n minus one applied as Bessel's correction for the spent degree of freedom.

visual.note: Bessel's correction uses n-1, not n

beats.text: The mean is not robust: a single outlier drags it far while the median barely flinches, the first lesson of robust statistics.
The breakdown point measures how much data you can corrupt before a statistic fails: zero percent for the mean, fifty for the median.
When you do not yet trust your data, summarize it with the median and the interquartile range before anything else.
With two variables, the first question is whether they move together, and covariance answers it directly by multiplying paired deviations.

visual.sub: Do X and Y move together?

beats.text: Covariance averages the product of signed deviations: same side of the mean gives positive, opposite sides give negative.
Covariance carries the units of X times Y, so rescaling metres to centimetres multiplies it by a hundred without changing anything real.

visual.label: spurious covariance factor from a metre-to-cm rescale

beats.text: Dividing covariance by the two standard deviations yields Pearson r, a unitless number locked to the interval from minus one to plus one.
Pearson sees only straight lines: it reports roughly zero for a clean parabola, a perfect but non-linear relationship.
With covariance six and standard deviations two and six, r equals six over twelve, a moderate positive association of one half.
Instead of asking whether points fall on a line, rank correlation asks whether they move in the same order.
Spearman replaces every value by its rank then runs ordinary Pearson on the ranks, so no outlier can pull harder than one rank.

visual.note: d_i is the difference of the two ranks

beats.text: Spearman measures monotonicity, not linearity, reaching plus one for the exponential or the logarithm where Pearson reports less.
Kendall's tau counts ordered pairs, subtracting discordant from concordant and dividing by the total number of pairs.
On strictly increasing curves Spearman hits plus one exactly while Pearson sags below one wherever the curve bends.
A large gap between Pearson and Spearman is itself a diagnostic: it signals non-linearity or outliers, so go look at the scatter plot.
A strong r with a tiny p-value still does not prove that X causes Y, and no amount of additional data fixes that gap.
A single correlation is consistent with four worlds: X causes Y, Y causes X, a confounder causes both, or pure coincidence.
The confounder is the most dangerous: a hidden Z drives both X and Y, manufacturing a correlation where no direct link exists.

visual.note: no arrow from X to Y, yet they correlate

beats.text: With unit variances and a equal to b equal to one, the shared driver alone produces a spurious correlation of one half.

visual.label: spurious corr(X,Y) from a confounder Z alone

beats.text: Simpson's paradox is the special case where a trend in every subgroup reverses once the groups are pooled together.
Berkeley admitted about forty-four percent of men and thirty-five of women overall, yet women fared equal or better department by department.

concepts.title: Causal thinking: DAGs, backdoor paths, the do-operator

beats.text: What turns correlation into causation is a causal model: an explicit, falsifiable claim about which variable affects which.
Pearl draws these claims as a directed acyclic graph, where nodes are variables, arrows are direct effects, and no loop returns to itself.
Three structures share the same variables: only the fork confounds, the chain holds a mediator, and the collider is a trap.

visual.tex: \text{fork } X \leftarrow Z \rightarrow Y \quad\text{chain } X \rightarrow Z \rightarrow Y \quad\text{collider } X \rightarrow Z \leftarrow Y
visual.note: the arrows decide what to control

beats.text: Controlling for everything is wrong: you adjust for a fork, never for a mediator, and never open a collider by conditioning on it.
The do-operator sets X by intervention, and backdoor adjustment recovers that interventional distribution from observational data.

visual.tex: P\big(Y \mid \mathrm{do}(X = x)\big) = \sum_{z} P\big(Y \mid X = x,\, Z = z\big)\, P(Z = z)
visual.note: the bridge from correlation to causation

beats.text: A randomized trial physically performs the do, deleting every arrow into X, which is why it needs no graph to be valid.

visual.label: spurious corr after conditioning on Z
